Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Infectious Disease digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 23 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Infectious Disease.

23articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

Impact of intermittent preventive treatment for malaria in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and their combination on infant outcomes: A randomized controlled trial

Nankabirwa JI, Kakuru A, Nguyen AT et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Do different intermittent preventive treatment regimens for malaria in pregnancy affect infant outcomes up to 12 months of age?
Key Findings:
  • Clinical malaria incidence in infants was similar across all three maternal treatment groups (IPTp-SP, IPTp-DP, and IPTp-DP+SP), with incidence rate ratios of 1.04 and 0.93 respectively compared to IPTp-SP
  • Infants born to mothers receiving dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (alone or combined) had higher prevalence of wasting at birth (RR 2.25-2.30) compared to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine
  • Growth differences resolved by 3-6 months of age with no long-term impact on infant development
Clinical Relevance: This evidence supports that while IPTp-DP may cause transient neonatal growth effects, it does not compromise long-term infant health outcomes, relevant for UK clinicians advising pregnant women travelling to malaria-endemic areas.
Limitations: Study conducted in a single high-transmission setting, limiting generalisability to different malaria transmission intensities.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

Fosmanogepix treatment for coccidioidomycosis under an expanded access program

Cohen SH, Allyn P, Chow FC et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Case series (expanded access program)
Key Question: What is the efficacy and tolerability of fosmanogepix in patients with severe coccidioidomycosis?
Key Findings:
  • Overall success rate of 73% (8/11 patients) with fosmanogepix treatment
  • High success rate in coccidioidal meningitis (75%, 6/8 patients) and moderate success in non-meningeal disseminated disease (67%, 2/3 patients)
  • Treatment was well tolerated with median duration of 208 days
Clinical Relevance: This provides early evidence for a new antifungal class in treating severe coccidioidomycosis, particularly relevant for UK clinicians managing imported cases or treating patients with treatment-refractory disease where conventional antifungals have failed.
Limitations: Very small patient numbers (n=11) and lack of control group limit generalisability of findings.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

Electronic Clinical Quality Measures of Antibiotic Use in Hospitalized Adults with Uncomplicated Community-acquired Pneumonia

White AT, Goetz MB, Jones M et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Multi-centre validation study
Key Question: Can electronic clinical quality measures (eCQMs) reliably identify inappropriate antibiotic prescribing in hospitalised patients with uncomplicated community-acquired pneumonia?
Key Findings:
  • Two eCQMs identified excessive duration (>5 days) in 40-55% of patients and inappropriate broad-spectrum empiric therapy in 17-56% across participating hospitals
  • Both measures demonstrated high reliability (91-93%) and validity when compared to manual chart review (sensitivity 96%, specificity 92-93%)
  • eCQMs used only structured EHR data fields, maximising feasibility for routine implementation
Clinical Relevance: These validated electronic measures could support antimicrobial stewardship programmes in UK hospitals by providing automated surveillance of CAP prescribing practices without manual chart review.
Limitations: Study conducted in US healthcare systems with different prescribing patterns and EHR systems than the NHS.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

Ensitrelvir for the treatment of hospitalized adults with COVID-19: an international phase 3 randomized placebo-controlled trial

Baker JV, Siegel L, Losso M et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Randomized placebo-controlled trial
Key Question: Does ensitrelvir improve clinical recovery when added to standard care in hospitalized adults with COVID-19?
Key Findings:
  • No significant improvement in clinical recovery: median Days to Recovery Scale was 6 vs 5.5 (OR 0.82, 95% CI 0.62-1.09, p=0.19)
  • Reduced viral antigen detection at day 5 (13.4% vs 25.1%, p<0.001) but no clinical benefit
  • Higher mortality (6.1% vs 4.4%) and hemorrhagic events (3.4% vs 0.3%) in ensitrelvir group, though not statistically powered for these outcomes
Clinical Relevance: This large international trial suggests ensitrelvir offers no additional clinical benefit to current NHS standard care (corticosteroids plus remdesivir) for hospitalized COVID-19 patients in the Omicron era.
Limitations: Study conducted during lower-severity Omicron period with high background use of proven therapies, potentially limiting ability to detect additional benefit.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

A Phase 1/2 Dose-Ranging Safety and Immunogenicity Study of mRNA-Based Candidate Pandemic Influenza Vaccines in Healthy Adults

Cosgrove CA, Avanesov A, Rust D et al. · 2026 Apr 25
Study Type: Phase 1/2 dose-ranging RCT
Key Question: What are the safety, reactogenicity, and immunogenicity profiles of mRNA-based pandemic influenza vaccines (H5N8, H7N9, H5-only, H7-only) at different doses in healthy adults?
Key Findings:
  • Local adverse reactions occurred in 76.8% of 1,499 participants (mainly injection-site pain), with systemic reactions in 62.8% (fatigue, headache); most were grade 1-2 severity with no grade 4 events
  • Immune responses increased dose-dependently, with H5-based candidates showing stronger strain-specific HAI responses than H7-based candidates, though microneutralization titres were comparable
  • All vaccine candidates were well-tolerated across the dose ranges tested (12.5-100µg depending on formulation)
Clinical Relevance: These data support mRNA technology as a viable platform for rapid pandemic influenza vaccine development, relevant to UK pandemic preparedness planning given the ongoing avian influenza threat.
Limitations: Short follow-up period (205 days) limits assessment of longer-term immunity and safety profile.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

Updated hepatitis D seroprevalence among HBsAg-positive persons aged ≥6 years in the United States, National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, 2007‒2018

Casillas SM, Chida A, Drobeniuc J et al. · 2026 Apr 25
Study Type: Cross-sectional seroprevalence study
Key Question: What is the accurate hepatitis D virus (HDV) seroprevalence among HBsAg-positive individuals in the United States?
Key Findings:
  • HDV seroprevalence was 1.26% among HBsAg-positive participants aged ≥6 years using NHANES data from 2007-2018
  • This represents validation testing using a different assay methodology compared to previous estimates
  • Study covered an 11-year surveillance period providing robust population-level data
Clinical Relevance: This validates HDV co-infection rates in chronic hepatitis B patients, informing UK clinicians about expected prevalence rates for HDV screening in HBsAg-positive patients, particularly relevant given similar epidemiological patterns in Western countries.
Limitations: The abstract provides insufficient detail about the assay methodology used or comparison with previous estimates.
PubMed →DOI →
Nature reviews. Microbiology

Phage host range: determinants, dynamics and applications

Leprince A, Somerville V, Addablah AA et al. · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Review article
Key Question: What molecular and ecological factors determine bacteriophage host range and how do these influence phage-based therapeutic applications?
Key Findings:
  • Host range is determined by complex interactions beyond traditional phage-bacterial receptor binding, including bacterial defence systems, phage-phage interactions, and environmental factors
  • Host specificity is dynamic due to ongoing phage-bacterial coevolution and phenotypic variability
  • New genomic tools are being developed to predict phage host range at strain level, moving beyond traditional lysis-based assays
Clinical Relevance: Understanding phage host range determinants is crucial for optimising phage therapy applications in NHS settings, particularly for targeting specific bacterial pathogens while minimising disruption to beneficial microbiota.
Limitations: As a review article, findings depend on the quality and scope of underlying primary research studies.
PubMed →DOI →
The Journal of infectious diseases

Cryptosporidium parvum outbreak among volunteers of a licensed wildlife rehabilitation center housing raccoons-2024

Miko S, Austin C, Smith BL et al. · 2026 Apr 19
Study Type: Outbreak investigation
Key Question: Can raccoons serve as a source of zoonotic Cryptosporidium parvum transmission to humans in wildlife rehabilitation settings?
Key Findings:
  • 18 human cryptosporidiosis cases identified among volunteers at a single wildlife rehabilitation facility housing raccoons
  • Molecular subtyping (IIaA15G2R1) confirmed identical C. parvum strains in both human and raccoon specimens
  • This represents the first documented outbreak linking raccoons as a source of human cryptosporidiosis
Clinical Relevance: UK infectious disease clinicians should be aware that raccoons can transmit cryptosporidiosis to humans, expanding the recognised zoonotic reservoir beyond traditional ruminant sources—relevant for travel medicine consultations and potential future UK wildlife facility exposures.
Limitations: Small case series with limited molecular characterisation of specimens may not fully establish the direction or mechanism of transmission.
PubMed →DOI →
